rationale: AI-Scientist v2 performs literature search during ideation, not as a separate novelty gate. Treat this as provisional until a separate checker is added.

raw.Name: diversity_adaptive_guidance
raw.Title: Diversity-Adaptive Guidance for Diffusion Models: Conditioning Strength from Online Uncertainty and Repulsion
raw.Short Hypothesis: A major weakness of classifier-free guidance is that it applies a fixed conditioning strength across all timesteps and samples, even though over-guidance is known to reduce diversity and distort samples. The hypothesis is that guidance should be strongest only when the conditional signal is confident and weakly multimodal, and should be reduced when the denoiser indicates uncertainty or when a batch of samples begins to collapse toward similar trajectories. A simple adaptive rule that modulates guidance using online disagreement between conditional and unconditional predictions, plus a lightweight batch-level repulsion term in latent/noise space, can improve the fidelity-diversity tradeoff without retraining the base diffusion model. This is the right setting because guidance is already applied only at sampling time, so adaptation can be tested cheaply and cleanly; simpler alternatives like globally tuning the guidance scale cannot react to per-sample ambiguity or batch-level collapse.
raw.Related Work: Classifier-free guidance and related guidance methods improve conditional fidelity but often trade away diversity, mode coverage, and calibration. Subsequent work studies guidance schedules across timesteps, oversaturation from large guidance scales, fast samplers, consistency models, and various corrective heuristics. There is also broad work on diversity promotion in generative models, including truncation-style controls in GANs and anti-collapse regularization. This proposal is distinct in three ways: (1) it does not introduce a new conditional model, reward model, or expensive retraining objective; (2) it uses the geometry already exposed by classifier-free guidance—specifically the per-step conditional-unconditional discrepancy—as an online uncertainty proxy to adapt guidance per sample and per timestep; and (3) it adds a simple inference-time repulsion between simultaneously generated trajectories to discourage emergent batch-level collapse. Unlike timestep-only schedules, the method is data-dependent. Unlike training-time diversity regularizers or mixture-of-experts denoisers, it is a drop-in sampler modification. Unlike fast-sampler papers, the primary target is improved coverage at equal compute rather than fewer steps.
raw.Abstract: Diffusion models rely heavily on guidance to improve conditional sample quality, yet fixed guidance scales often induce a poor fidelity-diversity tradeoff: samples become sharp and on-condition but lose coverage, collapse toward stereotyped modes, or exhibit oversaturated artifacts. We propose Diversity-Adaptive Guidance (DAG), a training-free inference method that adjusts guidance strength online using signals already available in classifier-free guided diffusion. At each denoising step, DAG computes a per-sample uncertainty score from the discrepancy between conditional and unconditional predictions and uses it to attenuate guidance when the conditional direction appears unstable or excessively dominant. To further prevent multiple samples in a batch from following nearly identical denoising trajectories, DAG adds a lightweight repulsion term in latent/noise space that discourages trajectory crowding while preserving condition alignment. The method requires no auxiliary classifier, no retraining, and only modest overhead beyond standard sampling. We test DAG on class-conditional and text-conditional diffusion in lightweight academic settings, comparing against fixed-scale guidance and hand-designed timestep schedules. Evaluation emphasizes the Pareto frontier between fidelity and diversity using FID, precision/recall, intra-condition coverage metrics, CLIP alignment for text conditioning, and trajectory-dispersion diagnostics. We hypothesize that adaptive, uncertainty-aware guidance can recover substantial diversity lost by conventional guidance while retaining most of its fidelity benefits. If successful, the work would reframe diffusion guidance as a closed-loop control problem rather than a static scalar hyperparameter.
Experiments: Implement a baseline DAG sampler for classifier-free guidance: replace fixed scale s with s_t,i = s_max * g(u_t,i), where u_t,i is an uncertainty proxy for sample i at step t computed from the norm ratio or cosine disagreement between conditional and unconditional noise predictions. Test simple monotone functions g such as sigmoid attenuation or clipped inverse proportionality. Compare against fixed guidance and linear/cosine timestep schedules on CIFAR-10 class-conditional diffusion. Metrics: FID, improved precision/recall, class accuracy of generated samples, and entropy of predicted class labels for diversity.

Add batch-level repulsion: for K samples generated under the same condition, perturb each guided update with a small term that pushes trajectories apart in x_t or predicted x_0 space using nearest-neighbor repulsion. Evaluate whether repulsion improves intra-class coverage without harming fidelity. Metrics: FID, precision/recall, pairwise LPIPS diversity, coverage over pretrained feature clusters, and stability across random seeds.

Ablate uncertainty definitions: use conditional-unconditional norm gap, cosine angle, temporal variance of guidance direction over the last few steps, and small Monte Carlo dropout or noise perturbation disagreement if available. Measure which proxy best predicts eventual collapse or oversaturation. Metrics: correlation between proxy values and final sample quality/diversity, plus Pareto frontier area.

Test on a lightweight text-to-image setup such as a small latent diffusion model or a public pretrained model with reduced resolution. Compare prompt adherence and diversity under fixed CFG, timestep schedules, and DAG. Metrics: CLIP score, FID or zero-shot retrieval-based fidelity proxy, LPIPS diversity for same-prompt batches, and prompt-wise mode count estimated by clustering image embeddings.

Study compute-performance tradeoffs: vary number of sampling steps and assess whether DAG is more beneficial in low-step regimes where guidance mistakes are harder to recover. Compare DDIM/DPM-Solver style samplers with and without DAG. Metrics: FID/diversity curves versus wall-clock time and number of function evaluations.

Perform failure-case analysis on prompts/classes with intrinsic multimodality versus unambiguous conditions. Hypothesis: DAG should help most on ambiguous prompts or broad classes. Quantify gains separately on coarse versus fine-grained conditions using conditional embedding variance and feature-cluster coverage.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The uncertainty proxy derived from conditional-unconditional discrepancy may not reliably track true ambiguity; it may instead reflect scale differences unrelated to diversity loss.
Batch repulsion could create artificial diversity by pushing samples off-manifold, harming realism or prompt fidelity if too strong.

Some existing papers may already explore adaptive CFG schedules or anti-collapse heuristics; the novelty depends on clearly emphasizing per-sample closed-loop adaptation plus batch trajectory repulsion.
Benefits may be small for highly capable pretrained models or heavily optimized samplers, especially at large compute budgets.
Text-to-image evaluation of diversity is noisy and metric-sensitive; careful human or prompt-stratified analysis may still be needed.
The method assumes access to both conditional and unconditional predictions as in classifier-free guidance; it is less directly applicable to other guidance mechanisms.
